title: Questioning & Posing Problems: Is It Okay To Make a Mistake?
content:
Should we celebrate making mistakes?

Institute for Habits of Mind Co-Director Bena Kallick isn't so sure.

"I always love it when we say we celebrate mistakes, because I'm not sure it's a celebration," she laughs. "I don't usually feel it."

She goes on to explain that the celebration is actually around the sense that the mind is working, not that the mistake has been made.

"I'm not frozen, I'm actually willing to take a chance on myself," says Kallick. "Very often for people, especially when you're beating yourself up for a mistake that you made, you feel like, 'Maybe I should have just kept my mouth shut. Maybe I shouldn't have tried.'

"So it seems to me, it's the celebration of being willing to take a risk, to take a chance, to stay persistent, to not give up."

Co-Director Art Costa agrees, stating, "What you're celebrating is learning from the mistake. (Thomas) Edison found 10,000 ways how not to make a light bulb."

Costa says that, to change the language and the notion of mistake-making or failure-making, there must be a psychologically safe environment in which teachers are modeling what it looks like to make a mistake.

Watch the LinkedIn Live Conversation

"Teachers have to exhibit a vulnerability that they will admit the mistake," he said. "'Whoops, I sure blew that one.'"

Kallick, Costa, and Co-Director Allison Zmuda will be engaging in monthly LinkedIn Live conversations like this one about each of the Habits of Mind. To participate in future conversations, follow the Institute for Habits of Mind on LinkedIn.

title: Tapping Into the 5 Senses to Support Students With Disabilities
content:
By Daniel Vollrath

Activities that draw upon the five senses—such as spending time outdoors—can be centering for students in special education.

Our ability to smell, taste, hear, touch, and see plays an essential part in our everyday lives. These senses help us interpret thoughts and feelings about our surrounding environment and contribute to well-being. In the classroom, when students understand how to address each sense, they are more aware of what’s happening around them and within their body and mind.

Teachers who recognize the benefits of incorporating the five senses into the classroom can provide students with more than just awareness of their surroundings—they can also help support students’ mental health. As a teacher in a special education high school classroom, I have found that incorporating the five senses helps my students relieve anxiety and enhances their ability to focus and maintain attention.

8 STRATEGIES TO INCORPORATE THE SENSES INTO YOUR CLASSROOM

  1. Bring it all together. The 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 coping technique is designed to decrease anxiety and support overwhelming thoughts and actions. It can be used before, in the middle of, or at the end of class. Ask students to do the following:
    • Acknowledge five things you see around you.
    • Acknowledge four things you can touch around you.
    • Acknowledge three things you can hear.
    • Acknowledge two things you can smell.
    • Acknowledge one thing you can taste.
  2. Create a sensory workspace. This offers a self-soothing space for students to be effective and focused. The strategy begins by giving students the option of having a sensory item of their choice at their desks. Their selection can be based on the activity or assignment. For example, if students are reading silently, they could take out a stuffed animal to squeeze; or if they are taking a quiz, it might be helpful to have a bottle of water on their desk to stay hydrated.
  3. Provide sensory questions of the day. Create an opportunity for students to answer a question of the day about a specific sense. This strategy allows students to share their senses, which brings them comfort, joy, and connection to calm:
    • What is your favorite smell, and how does it make you feel?
    • What is the best-tasting beverage? When you drink it, what does it do to your attitude?
    • If you could touch anything that brings you happiness, what would it be? Why?
    • The best sound in the world is ____ because it allows me to ____.
    • What view brings you happiness?
  4. Have students complete a weekly or daily senses journal. Through this strategy, students keep track of their senses, their experiences, and how the senses make them feel. The sense can be something from the weekend or throughout their day that they notice.

    For example, a student once wrote about a restaurant he had gone to over the weekend. In his journal, he described the smell of the restaurant, the taste of the burger he ate, and the great music being played at dinner. This memory elicited positive feelings and offered a relaxing thought for this student. I could tell that this reflection put him in a positive mood. Keeping a journal allows students to check in with a motivating thought to focus or relieve anxiety when needed.
  5. Smile. The mindful smile strategy can be mood-changing! Smiling can increase your mood along with others around you. The sight of a smile directed to each student as they walk throu

title: Emphasizing Growth, Not Grades, in Parent-Teacher Conferences
content:
You can involve students and their parents or guardians in conferences by focusing on growth and goal-setting.

By Lory Walker Peroff and first posted on Eduplanet.

Conferences are a special time to connect with parents and guardians. However, too often, parents and students are more concerned about grades than about the learning that takes place to earn them.

Unfortunately, by making the report card the star of the show, traditional models of parent-teacher conferences may unwittingly reinforce this shortsighted notion that grades are more important than learning. It is time for teachers to use conferences to reclaim the narrative on learning.

Here are four ideas I use to shift the focus of conferences from grades to growth.

1. START WITH AN ICEBREAKER

Conferences can be nerve-wracking for all parties. Why not start with a fun icebreaker? Provide the parent with a note card, and ask them to provide three adjectives to describe their child. On a separate note card, write three adjectives to describe the child from your perspective as their teacher. Then, invite all parties to share their words.

After sharing positive adjectives, I’ve witnessed parents’ eyes light up and demeanors soften. Very often, some adjectives match, which provides reassurance to the parent that the teacher really knows their child.

Another fun and easy icebreaker is to draw the future. We remember things better when we draw them. Ask parents to take two minutes to sketch a picture of an ideal future for the rest of their child’s academic year. Ask the student to do the same. Then share, compare, and discuss. This is a terrific jumping-off point to honor student dreams and jointly make a plan to move toward achieving them.

To save time, you can assign these activities as "pre-work" for parents and classwork for students prior to conference time. Doing so offers low-lift, student-centered ways to set the tone that conferences are about the whole child, not just marks on a progress report.

2. MAKE IT A TEAM EFFORT

The week before the conference, send home a "Grows and Glows" sheet for parents to fill out, helping them reflect on their child’s strengths and areas of concern prior to the conference.

Inviting parents into the discussion makes communication about learning a two-way street. Not only do parents appreciate the opportunity to think and share about their child, but also this strategy provides useful insight into home behaviors and attitudes. Parents are proud to share their child’s strengths. They are equally eager to collaborate on how to address areas of need both academic and social. By building a bridge between home and school, students, parents, and teachers are more likely to work together to harmoniously support student learning goals and general well-being.

3. STUDENT-LED CONFERENCE

Shift the focus from grades to growth by transferring the responsibility of sharing progress from teachers to students. Empower students to lead their own conferences by inviting them to create a slide show to present to their parents.

Slides can include students’ self-identified areas of strength, areas for growth, and goals for the upcoming quarter. This is a terrific way to put students in control of sharing their learning by identifying "proud pieces," "growth pieces," or other artifacts that support their self-reflections. It also provides an opportunity for students to practice presentation skills.

For younger students, you may want to provide a slide template—which also provides a strategic introduction to growth mindset vocabulary by allowing you to share phrases like "still learning" and "hasn’t mastered yet." This helps families understand that learning is not always linear, encapsulated by a single letter grad

title: Putting Habits of Mind Into Practice with Montecito Union
content:
On April 5th and 6th, we will be gathering at the Vista Unified School District in southern California for the Habits of Mind Conference!

Register NOW for Early Bird Pricing!

The day before –Thursday, April 4th – attendees are invited for a pre-conference site visit to Montecito Union, an IHOM Learning Community of Excellence, so that they can see what the Habits of Mind look like and feel like in action.

During our recent live Q&A with Nick Bruski and Rusty Ito, Montecito Union's Principal and Assistant Principal, Nick explained that Montecito Union's mission statement is to "launch inspired thinkers who will positively impact the world."

"As all the latest research is coming out," he said, "kids need so much more of the softer skills to be successful in their lives, to be problem solvers, to be critical thinkers, to be partners, to be kind, to have respect and integrity. And that, you know, we wanted a more formalized curriculum to really educate our students and prepare them to be productive citizens in life outside of the classroom."

"As a school, we made a really conscious effort to really promote integration in all senses of the word."

How do Montecito Union Public Schools integrate the Habits of Mind?

Monthly "Roadmaps"

Each month focuses on a specific Habit. Monthly classroom meetings focus around that Habit and other skill sets to empower learners to be not only academically successful, but socially and emotionally successful as well.

Integration Into Curriculum

The Habits are very intentionally integrated into the curriculum at all age levels. For example, when kindergarteners read The Little Engine That Could, the story is connected to Persistence; science is tied to Responding with Wonderment and Awe; in math, Striving for Accuracy and Precision is emphasized as well as Communicating with Accuracy and Precision (showing your work).

"When teachers are empowered–everyone at your school, kindergarten through sixth grade, is sending messages about being accurate and precise, and communicating their thinking clearly–when those kids get to sixth grade, they look a lot different than a child who didn't have six prior years of those two Habits interwoven with math consistently."

https://youtu.be/nFUDRycZdqI?si=1jk3-w5hJxJxleSW

Integration Into Assessment

Five core Habits are graded each semester and included as part of the "thinking and learning dispositions" segment of Montecito Union's report cards. Students also use the Habits of Mind as self reflection tools. Rusty explained:

"Sometimes that self reflection aligns with the teachers' assessment, and sometimes it doesn't, but then again, great conversation piece and partnership with the families. A great opportunity to say: 'Here's what's successful, here's where we can grow, let's put a plan together to make that happen.' "

Extracurricular Interactions

Organic opportunities often arise during recess and lunch, and other less structured times–Managing Impulsivity, Thinking Interdependently, and Thinking Flexibly all come up in the nature of play and conflict resolution. These are perfect learning opportunities to connect real life skills and the Habits together as opportunities for growth.

Modeling The Language, Particularly Around Tough Conversations

Nick relayed the story of a misunderstanding that was caused by unclear co

title: Teacher Perceptions on Infusing Habits of Mind
content:
Infused water ... probably one of the latest trends you’ve likely seen, tried, or even heard about.

What is not to love about fresh water infused with your favorite fruit, vegetable, herb, etc., the list can go on. This concept of flavorful water is not something new or out of the ordinary. In fact, for many years people have been enjoying a lemon in their water, a lime in a cocktail, and a cherry in a Shirley Temple. Additionally, and even more rewarding, is the health benefits of drinking infused water.

Research from a variety of sources indicates that drinking naturally flavored water keeps the body hydrated, improves skin, facilitates the transportation of nutrients and oxygen, lubricates joints, and increases energy within muscles.

Wow ... think about it, the infusion of a fruit, and water, can be that impactful! You have me hooked ... I am now motivated and focused on making a change for the better.

Now, let’s think about another type of infusion. One that is not physically performed, but instead naturally produced through the actions of productive behaviors and learning.

This intermingling we are referring to includes the Habits of Mind with instructional teaching and learning.

Now, you may ask yourself, why is this infusion so unique and critical to the needs of learners? Think about it, not only are curricular demands being met (objectives, standards, units, goals, etc.), but now present are the situational opportunities for learning to occur via skillful and strategic behaviors within the classroom.

With this infusion idea in place, we set the tone for students to perform these effective dispositions within their learning; while the ultimate goal is for students to be able to present these behaviors in and outside of the classroom.

To show the true effects of the Habits of Mind infused into pedagogical experiences, teacher perceptions will be presented below.

What Do the Teachers Say?

"The Habits of Mind are very focused on opportunity to be reflective. When the school day is over I find myself more reflective about my lesson for the day, whether students understood the material, and about the behaviors they presented in the classroom. I never thought about the behaviors until I started using the Habits of Mind in the classroom tied together with lesson plans. More and more I am able to see the importance of students habits in connection to the work they produce."

Science Teacher

The aspect of reflection seems to be the most crucial component for this science teacher.

As most teachers should be reflective about their practice, the habits seem to evoke a new piece of thinking not really touched upon, that being the "productive behaviors" component.

Most notable was how the teacher was able to recognize a relationship between habits and the impact they brought forth within students’ produced work. The ability to identify habits within students’ development of projects, assignments, and assessments is an element that teachers often fail to connect; yet, these habits are the pieces that bring forth the success, skills, and behaviors we desire from our students.

In another reflective journal the science teacher made a connection to the teacher evaluation process the school follows, Danielson’s Framework for Teaching and Learning. Here is what she wrote:

"I find the Habits of Mind connect so much to the framework of Danielson’s model for teaching. It ties in so well with what we should be doing as teachers, that being reflective. As a teacher I analyze my pedagogy, reflect on daily instruction, model behaviors, and focus on the building of a culture for learning. These are all "habits" that I am focusing on. So really I am building and di

title: 7 Habits to Boost Productivity and Well-Being in a Complex Profession
excerpt: An article from REALTOR® Magazine written by Dan Vollrath
content:
You have the power to be more innovative, responsive and effective. Here’s how.

\n

You’re in a rewarding profession—helping people realize a significant life goal. But selling real estate comes with distinct challenges. While constantly working to fill your pipeline, manage client expectations, and achieve a sense of work-life balance, you’re likely to face moments of financial uncertainty, performance pressure and market volatility, as well as feelings of isolation and loneliness. As an educator with a mom who has been in the business 40 years, I’ve seen these challenges up close. So what can you do to counteract those stressors?

Consciously drive for productivity. Productivity can create a positive feedback loop in which success leads to increased confidence and motivation, further reducing stress and promoting a healthy work-life balance.

What helps you be most productive might be different from what works for others. For some, it’s setting aside time each day for professional development or to contact clients with updates. For others, the best productivity tool might not be work-related. You might get a boost from a breathing exercise, taking a walk or meditating. To be more productive and stay in the moment, try these seven strategies.

  1. Listen actively
  2. Remain open to continuous learning
  3. Strive for accuracy
  4. Communicate clearly and precisely
  5. Think flexibly
  6. Manage impulsivity
  7. Persist

Read the full article on the National Association of Realtors® website.

title: 7 Tools for Developing Purpose-Driven Students​
excerpt: The beginning of the school year is a great time to encourage students to plan with their future selves in mind.
content:
This article was originally published on the Edutopia blog. Read the full article.

For many children, the shift in mindset from summer break to school can be a tough transition. It’s as if children are flipping on a switch in their brains from their relaxed summer selves to a focused and motivated self. 

This new self will face responsibilities, expectations, and deadlines for assignments, projects, and upcoming assessments. Let’s face it, this can all be overwhelming. However, for teachers, finding ways to build a purpose-driven classroom offers students opportunities to envision their future selves.

Encouraging students to develop their future selves plays a pivotal role in school and life. When students jump from one grade level to the next, they often do not consider the importance of personal growth and self-expectations; instead, the focus is more on what the teacher and school curriculum require.

One way to embrace a new school year is to come in with purpose and commitment toward developing your future self. Your future self is best when crafted and thought about at the beginning of the school year. Whether it be the first couple of days of school or throughout the year, engaging your students in being purpose-driven offers a valuable skill.

1. Self Intention Letters

An intention is a commitment to oneself. Writing a letter to yourself where you articulate your intentions—goals, motivations, plans for the future school year, etc.—can elicit introspection and forward thinking.

2. Scorecards.

Create a scorecard with three to five actions, habits, or skills you will incorporate into your future self. Each day, you will check off whether you completed the action or not. For instance, you might create four actions to incorporate into your school day.

3. Daily routines.

No one routine fits everyone’s mold. Routines are meant to be tailored to each individual. Setting a daily routine can support students in being more productive, focused, and motivated. A routine can be incorporated in and outside of the classroom.

4. Habits awareness checklists.

Developing habits is essential in building progress in life and learning. A habit differs from a routine in that it involves a cue that triggers a certain behavior or action. For example, before you go to bed (cue), your brain triggers the habitual act of having to brush your teeth. Over time, this behavior becomes automatic and often is repeated subconsciously. 

A routine, however, is a series of planned intentional actions. As students fast-forward their thinking into the next school year, offer the opportunity to think about what habits they would like to improve or consider being more aware of in school.

5. Operator's manuals.

An operator’s manual is a way for students to provide information about how they operate—both effectively and ineffectively—within the learning environment. It’s an opportunity for students to not only reflect but provide a mini-biography about themselves as learners to their teachers. You can create specific categories, or students can create their own. A few categories might be limitations, strengths, weaknesses, stressors, recovery techniques, energizers, and so on.

6. Awareness of personal peak, trough, and recovery times.

Much research has been done on the importance of when we are most and least productive during the day. Daniel Pink, a best-selling author and renowned researcher on motivation, discusses the importance of time in his book When: The Scientific Secrets of Perfect Timing.

7. Elevator pitches.

An elevator pitch—most common in the business world—is a short, memorable description of what you do or sell. It should be no longer than 30 seconds and easily digestible for the listener.

For Daniel Vollrath's full descriptions of each of the tools, read the full article on Edutopia.

About Daniel Vollrath

Daniel Vollrath is a special education teacher at Hunterdon Central Regional High School in New Jersey and a Habits of Mind Consultant. With a strong passion for developing curriculum, building student relationships, and fostering executive function techniques infused with the Habits of Mind, Vollrath has created a more

title: How to Integrate the Habits of Mind from PreK Through 12th Grade
excerpt: "The Habits of Mind are not a separate curriculum. They\
content:
You may be looking to integrate the 16 Habits of Mind into your classroom, but you're facing overwhelm: how do you choose which Habits of Mind you spotlight with your students?

In a recent LIVE Q&A with Habits of Mind Consultants Michelle Hughes and Daniel Vollrath, Habits of Mind Co-Director Bena Kallick asked exactly that question.

"I know you created a playbook that somewhat helps us to answer that question," she prompted, "but how do teachers answer that question? How do they choose which one? How do they get started?"

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CO2f4LLC4u8

The Habits of Mind in High School

Michelle and Dan shared insights they each highlighted in the Habits of Mind Playbook, available within the Habits of Mind Shop. Dan, a special education teacher at Hunterdon Central Regional High School in New Jersey, said he introduces all 16 Habits of Mind to his class before selecting 4-5 to focus on throughout the year.

"I think it's very important to know your students, to know the classroom culture, and to design the habits around the need within the classroom," he said. "Sixteen is a lot. However, if you space them out throughout the whole year that could be effective."

Through his Habits of Mind Checklist, Dan encourages his students to take note of their relationship with four habits selected at the outset of the year. Students will also journal about those habits in addition to others the class isn't focusing on.

"For example, if we had Persisting, Striving for Accuracy, Managing Impulsivity and Thinking Flexibly as our four for the class, they can step out of that comfort zone in their journaling and write about a different Habit of Mind."

The Habits of Mind in Elementary

Michelle, who compiled the Habits of Mind Playbook, discussed this practice with younger students, PreK - fourth grade. One thing she did early in the development of the playbook was look at the Habits of Mind that were appropriate for younger students.

"Responding with Wonderment, and Awe, which they do naturally, Gathering Data Through all the Senses," she said, "and kids are very sensory. They're very aware of what they're seeing, hearing, feeling, touching.

"Remaining Open to Continuous Learning, and Questioning and Problem Posing, which we know they naturally do."

As students get older -- second through fourth grade -- the concept of analyzing their relationship to specific Habits of Mind is woven into the process.

"Which Habits do they find they're naturally good at?" Michelle asked, "Which Habits do they sometimes use because they are in certain situations? Which Habits do they want to grow and why?"

If a school district is really working on the Habits of Mind and wants to develop them, this practice continues to move developmentally throughout the grades until students graduate high school.

To learn more about the program, visit the Habits of Mind Playbook page.

title: Poetry: Metacognitive Hero of the New
excerpt: Poetry itself is not a form of writing. It is a form of THOUGHT.
content:
Poetry is one of the most undervalued vehicles today used to enhance metacognitive thought within the classroom. It’s understandable. Most teachers (not all—but yes, most) do not understand how to incorporate poetry into their lesson constructs, or they just flat out do not understand it.

On top of that, one of the biggest mistakes teachers make when teaching poetry is teaching structured poetry. This is an especially significant mistake when the first experience we give students (typically at the elementary level) is structured rhymed poetry—including the usage of acrostic.

The reason these types of structured verse do such a disservice to students early on is that they put a limitation on thought itself. Poetry becomes a task in finding words to fit into a structure or form rather than allowing students to explore their thinking free of the chains of formal verse or syllable.

There is a huge difference.

For a moment, pretend to forget everything you have previously learned about poetry. Throw it all out the window and start over. Start fresh. Start NEW. Every single step we take in the world of poetry should be different and new. That is the beautiful thing about it. Poetry involves manifesting new perspectives and ideas to the world—things the world has never seen or even considered.

First of all, the foundations of poetry do not lay in writing.

So let’s get that straight right off the bat. Poetry is about IDEAS. It’s about LIFE. It is about creating your own ideas about LIFE. NEW ideas. Yes, we obviously know that in order to convey your ideas through a poem, writing is involved.

But consider this. Poetry itself is not a form of writing. It is a form of THOUGHT.

To be a good poet means that you are able to understand LIFE—to analyze its intricacies, its wonders, its secrets, its overall patterns, and be able to articulate these things for others to understand. This is what we want our students to be isn’t it? Wonderers? Questioners? Analyzers? Engagers in the world around them? Dissectors of the past day’s events—AND—providing meaning or significance to them?

Start with free verse when you teach poetry.

Forget the limericks and forget the acrostics. Forget the sonnets and forget the haikus. Free verse is where it’s at, baby. Free. I mean it’s right in there connected with the word “verse”, right? Your mind is FREE. No chains. No restrictions. Your students are free to focus on thought itself. 

New thought. 

New ideas. 

Analyze concepts such as “knowledge” or “art”. Analyze beauty such as “waterfall” or “rain forest”. Analyze the mundane such as “pencil”, “student”, or “truck”. Look at all these things in a brand new way and make your reader see these things in a brand new way as well.

As you get your students to start up their poetic engines, it is time to get them to take responsible risks. Writing is typically voted the “least liked” subject area of the core subjects. So taking a risk if you think you are a poor writing is essential. Never criticize during the first few times your students share some of their work and do not offer advice.

Instead, highlight and applaud when a student writes something that gives you a new outlook on the subject. And when you do this, your class as a whole will begin to take more risks as well. They will slowly begin to see what you mean by “NEW”.

As time goes on, you can sprinkle in mini lessons on various “literary weapons” (as I call them) which slowly begin to build your students’ poetic arsenal. Introduce simile, metaphor, personification, and alliteration.

Show them exceptional examples from literature and classic poetry where these techniques are used and why they are effective. Invite students to take more responsible risks in attempting new techniques and applaud success.

Applaud risk!

As time continues to move, constantly challenge students to dissect the world at the metacognitive level.

  • What do you feel about this situation? How will you react to it?
  • What changes can you make to this world and why?
  • What is the purpose of a waterfall in the forest? Why is it there? If it could speak, what would it be saying to us?
  • What do you become (metaphorically speaking) when you are sad? When you are excited?

Poetry becomes a gateway for the mind to dissect the world in myriad ways and it gives our students a springboard into their meta-emotional thinking as well.

As teachers, we do not want our students to learn OF knowledge.

In other words, effective educators do not spend their days pouring random facts into students and moving on quickly.

title: Embracing Continuous Learning: Insights from the Habits of Mind Co-Directors
excerpt: "Being a human being is to be a continuous learner. We never stop learning."
content:
The Lifelong Commitment to Learning

In a fast-paced world where information is constantly changing, the ability to embrace continuous learning is more crucial than ever. 

This was the central theme in a recent conversation among the co-directors of Habits of Mind: Allison Zmuda, Bena Kallick, and Art Costa. Their discussion illuminated the essence of fostering a culture of learning—not just for students, but for everyone.

“Being a human being is to be a continuous learner. We never stop learning,” Costa stated, making it clear that this journey of learning is a lifelong commitment.

The trio emphasized that the only time we stop learning is when we’re no longer here, underscoring the idea that every experience can contribute to our growth.

The Dangers of Expertise

One of the standout points in their conversation was the danger of becoming too comfortable in our expertise. Kallick pointed out that a sense of certainty can often lead to stagnation.

“Ambiguity leaves us with a search, a quest we want to know more about,” she said.

This sentiment resonates with anyone who has ever felt stuck in their knowledge. It serves as a reminder that there’s always more to explore beyond what we think we know.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f4GmGOickPE&t=50s

Celebrating Mistakes

Humility also took center stage in their discussion. Kallick posed a thought-provoking question about the “humility of not knowing,” suggesting that acknowledging our gaps in knowledge is a strength, not a weakness.

Costa chimed in, highlighting how society often fears making mistakes, which can hinder our learning.

“We should celebrate our mistakes instead, turning them into valuable lessons,” he said. “It’d be fun to have a mistake meeting every once in a while,” he joked, imagining a space where people could laugh at their blunders and learn from them.

The Power of Collaboration

The conversation seamlessly transitioned into the importance of collaboration in learning. Costa and Kallick both pointed out that learning is richer when done in groups.

“Kids alone do not learn nearly as much as kids do in groups,” Costa remarked.

This idea of collective learning is not just applicable to classrooms; it’s relevant in all areas of life. When we work together, we can challenge each other’s thinking and deepen our understanding.

Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!

April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY

Learn More!

Introspection and Self-Discovery

Zmuda added another layer to this discussion by emphasizing introspection.

“The stories we tell ourselves can sometimes trap us in our own expertise,” she noted, encouraging everyone to keep an open mind about self-discovery.

This introspective approach is just as important as engaging with the world around us.

Staying Open in a Polarized World

In today’s often polarized climate, the trio agreed that staying open to continuous learning is not just a good idea—it’s essential.

“Remaining open means acknowledging differing opinions without becoming entrenched,” Zmuda pointed out.

This perspective is vital for fostering healthy conversations and understanding in our communities.

A Hope for Humanity

As they wrapped up their discussion, Costa expressed a heartfelt wish for humanity.

“I wish the world would continue to learn how to live in peace,” he said.

Kallick echoed this sentiment, emphasizing that continuous learners appreciate diverse perspectives, which fosters a growth mindset.

Conclusion

The insights shared by Zmuda, Kallick, and Costa serve as a refreshing reminder of the power of continuous learning. By remaining curious, embracing humility, and collaborating with others, we can enrich not only our own lives but also contribute to a more compassionate and informed society.

So, whether you’re a student, educator, or just someone eager to learn, remember: the journey of continuous learning is a lifelong adventure waiting to be explored.

title: Unlocking the Power of Habits of Mind: Transformative Stories from the Individual Practitioner Pathway
excerpt: Read powerful insights and personal stories about how the Habits of Mind have transformed their professional practices and personal lives through the Individual Practitioner Pathway
content:
Bena Kallick, Co-Founder of the Habits of Mind, recently hosted a live interview on the Habits of Mind social channels, featuring Dina Dormer, Debra Barry, and Janice Mix. These three educators shared powerful insights and personal stories about how the Habits of Mind have transformed their professional practices and personal lives through the Individual Practitioner Pathway.

A Pathway to Self-Awareness and Growth

For Dina Dormer, joining the Habits of Mind Individual Practitioner Pathway was more than professional development—it was a journey of self-discovery. Reflecting on her experience, Dormer shared how it reshaped her approach to both her personal and professional life.

"I realized a lot of what I was trying to do is just go, go, go, and put everything out there to maximize the student experience," Dormer said. "Through the practitioner program, I became much more aware and started to value the experience I was having along with the students."

Dormer emphasized how the Habits supported her during a time of significant transition in her career. "The impact that the Habits had on that transition and what I'm experiencing now with another kind of round of change has been essential," she said. "It’s helped me develop the skills and dispositions to handle changes, persist, and remain open to continuous learning."

One habit stood out for Dormer as a surprising area for growth. "I thought I was pretty good at listening with understanding and empathy," she said. "But when I moved into a supervisory role, I realized not so much. Learning to listen deeply and think interdependently has made a huge impact on my ability to work effectively with others."

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t63WvLV8veg

Managing Impulsivity in Everyday Life

Debra Barry’s story highlights how the Habits of Mind extend beyond the classroom, transforming personal behaviors and decisions. For her, managing impulsivity became a key focus.

"One of my first projects was titled 'Running to Nowhere,' and that’s exactly how I felt—always busy but not getting anywhere," Barry said. "I realized I wasn’t modeling the habits I was teaching to my students and colleagues."

Barry recounted a late-night moment that brought her learning to life. "It was 11 o'clock, and I decided to move a piece of exercise equipment up the stairs by myself," she said. "As I started, I thought, 'Debra, you’ve got to manage your impulsivity.' I stopped, realizing I’d either put a hole in the wall or hurt myself."

This shift in mindset became a turning point. "I used to see the Habits of Mind as a luxury I couldn’t afford," Barry said. "Now I know they are a necessity for my personal and professional growth."

A Cocktail of Habits for Connection and Understanding

Janice Mix, another practitioner, described how combining various Habits of Mind—her "cocktail of habits"—enabled her to better connect with both students and colleagues.

"Sometimes I was quick to think I understood what a student was saying," Mix said. "Now I’ve learned to take a step back and say, 'Tell me more about what you’re trying to say.' Nine times out of ten, they’re on the mark—it’s just that their thinking is a little different than mine. And that’s beautiful."

This approach has also enhanced her interactions with adults. "We all want to be heard when we’re speaking," Mix said. "Using this habit with adults allows me to understand much better, whether it’s with colleagues or even my own family."

Mix highlighted how the Habits provide a framework for managing stress and fostering resilience. "Just knowing I have something to draw on helps me navigate challenges," she said. "It’s about staying the course and persisting, even when it’s not easy."

title: Are You a Gongoozler at Heart? Cultivating Wonderment and Awe in Learning
excerpt: Learn how fostering the habit of responding with wonderment and awe can enhance student engagement, reflection, and curiosity in the classroom.
content:
Once in a rare while, you scan your emails or social media feeds and stumble upon an intriguing title for an article or blog. You click the link to read more and then you are transported.

It started with Allison as she reviewed the Fast Company newsletter over her morning coffee, intrigued by the use of story in the title "5 Questions to Discover the Story of Your own Success." So she opened up the link and was transported.

So she immediately shared the article with Bena with a cryptic message:

Are you a gongoozler at heart?

Bena leapt in to figure out what a gongoozler means and what the fuss was all about.

Gongoozle (gon·goo·zel/ gahn-goo-zel) verb: To stand and stare idly at boats moving through a canal or watercourse

She wondered:

How often do I just stand and stare idly? Just look at the leaves changing color or a lonely basil plant not yet frozen for the winter? Am I gongoozling when I replay moments of the past, consider where I will be in the day, and where that might take me in the future. What awakens me and insists that I be fully present with my interactions with the environment, other people, new ideas and curiosities? What matters most to me? How will what I am doing have an impact on those I care most about?

So, she realized the fuss about gongoozling is not so much in answers but in a thought provoking set of questions.

As we shared our personal reflections with one another, we imagined how gongoozling opened up fresh thinking for both of us and wanted to imagine how to frame this with our school clients.

How might it help to tell the stories of the curriculum journeys teachers were designing with the hope that the students would stop and idle long enough to reflect on what piqued their interest, curiosity, fascination?

To gongoozle in this way invites students to step into the river of ideas, searching for stories they might tell themselves and  others in a quest to make their journey meaningful and memorable.

Could they begin to tell where they have been in their learning journey so far? Would they allow themselves the patience to reflect without any artificial suggestions about what they should be reflecting on? 

Doing so focuses and strengthens the habit of Responding with Wonderment and Awe, tapping into our experiences. 

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jgXvumJ_1FI

Art and Bena illustrate what this habit looks, sounds, and feels like:

Young children are naturally curious. They commune with the world around them; they reflect on the changing formations of a cloud; feel charmed by the opening of a bud; sense the logical simplicity of mathematical order. They find beauty in a sunset, intrigue in the geometrics of a spider web, and exhilaration at the iridescence of a hummingbird’s wings. They see the congruity and intricacies in the derivation of a mathematical formula; recognize the orderliness and adroitness of a chemical change, and commune with the serenity of a distant constellation. And they never stop asking "why?"

If you are interested in diving deeper into the habit, Check out an interview with Art, Bena, and Allison specifically on Responding with Wonderment and Awe.

When designing a curriculum storyboard, open up wonderment with your students by:

  • Creating an experience that students are expected to stop, idle, and wonder about.
  • Sharing with students how what you have created piques your own interest, curiosity, and fascination.
  • Introducing them to the term gongoozle as another way of describing the habit of responding with wonderment and awe.
  • Co-creating a way for the class to share their responses through words, images, or another form that captures the moment.

And let us know if “gongoozle” becomes a new vocabulary word and sparks a T shirt design!

About the Habits

title: How the Habits of Mind Empower Schools Through Distributed Leadership and Co-Creation
content:
As a leader: learning from the past, living in the present and simultaneously imagining the future is an awesome task these days.

Every day we are flummoxed, humbled and in awe of all the changes that have amplified in their intensity opening new possibilities as well as new cautionary concerns. As a result, leaders around the world are turning to the Habits of Mind to inspire their teams to work collaboratively to build a vibrant and responsive school culture.

Recently, I worked with a principal who wanted to thoughtfully integrate Habits of Mind into the school culture. He also wanted to inspire his team by inviting them to co-create a plan for realizing that goal. I consulted with this school leader to help clarify where and how decision making would take place.

The principal was clear that this was a decision that he made -- he was not asking whether they wanted to integrate Habits of Mind. However, he did want them to play an active role in how the staff should do it.

Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!

April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY Learn More!

The day was designed through structured steps that would gradually increase the teacher's voice in their leadership decisions. This first step to distributing leadership was to set up a meeting with the school leadership team in order to design a full day workshop with the whole staff with the following commitments:

  • Everyone will have an opportunity to explore the Habits of Mind
  • Teachers can choose the three Habits of Mind they all agree would have the most power to introduce to the school. They will come to an agreement of this choice by consensus.
  • Teachers will have a plan for how to start the first 3 months of school by introducing the Habits of Mind in some way

A Window into the Professional Development Experience

Based on the three commitments, here is the flow of the session for staff to incorporate Habits of Mind into their personal and school experiences.
  1. Warm up to get acquainted with the HOM through their lived experiences. The questions posed for small group interaction were:
    • Which habit comes naturally to you and how does it affect you?
    • Which habit might you need most this year and why
    • Which habit would you most like to see your students demonstrate this year? Why?
  2. They brainstormed ways they might incorporate Habits of Mind into various areas of school life important to them.
    1. After sharing stories with one another, they set about choosing the three Habits of Mind. This activity was structured for consensus by small groups meeting to name three first; then charts to discover commonalities of choice; finally, a discussion about the few that were not high on agreement. These discussions led to further understanding about how and why the habits should be chosen. They chose: Listening with Understanding and Empathy, Thinking Flexibly, and Applying Past Knowledge to New Situations.
    2. As a result of their deliberation, they decided to first introduce the habits in the school as a whole before they would start to integrate them into their curriculum and instructional design.
    3. Liz chose one habit out of the three chosen to use as a model. On a chart, the group collectively co

title: Cultivating Thoughtful Leadership: Using Habits of Mind with Your Board of Education
content:
Boards of Education play a critical role in shaping the system’s future. Their decisions affect not only the financial stability of the organization but also its mission, values, and culture. With this immense responsibility, board members must bring more than just strategic thinking to the table -- they must embody a set of thoughtful practices that ensure leading with empathy, interdependence, and innovation. The Habits of Mind, a set of 16 problem-solving skills developed by Arthur Costa and Bena Kallick, can be instrumental in guiding board members through the complex challenges of school governance.

Below are three essential Habits of Mind that, when put to use in the work of the Boards, can elevate and strengthen communication and problem solving:

  • Listening with Understanding and Empathy
  • Thinking Interdependently
  • Questioning and Posing Problems
These habits align closely with the National Association of Independent Schools (NAIS) Principles of Good Practice for Independent School Trustees and the National Association of State Boards of Education (NASBE), which emphasize the importance of collaboration, long-term planning, and speaking as one voice.

Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!

April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY Learn More!

Listening with Understanding and Empathy

A foundational principle of effective board leadership is creating space for others to share their thoughts. In a boardroom, where diverse opinions and perspectives are essential to making informed decisions, board members must practice the habit of Listening with Understanding and Empathy. This habit asks board members to engage with one another and avoid responding immediately or defending a position. Rather, they listen attentively and genuinely work to understand where others are coming from.

For example, a board member may have strong feelings about a particular budget item, but listening to a colleague’s concerns about the long-term impact on faculty salaries or student financial aid can open the door to a more comprehensive discussion. By practicing active listening, board members not only enhance their own decision-making but also foster an environment where all voices are valued. Listening builds trust and ensures that decisions are made with a full understanding of their implications.

In addition to listening, empathy is a critical habit for board members. School boards often have to balance the needs of various stakeholders, including students, parents, faculty, and the broader community. However, it’s important to remember that the Superintendent or Head of School is the board’s only employee. As such, board members need to understand the immense responsibility the superintendent carries and be empathetic to the pressures that come with leading a district.

When board members approach decisions with empathy—whether it’s about tuition rates, programmatic changes, or even school policies—they are better able to align with the school’s core values. Empathy allows board members to consider the perspectives of all those affected by their decisions while maintaining a clear focus on the school’s long-term goals. This balance of empathy and strategic thinking is what enables board members to make compassionate yet mission-driven decisions.

Thinking Interdependently

Boards are most effective when they function as cohesive units, with each board member understanding how their actions impact the larger system. The habit of thinking interdependently aligns clos

title: Embracing Radical Optimism: 2025 Habits of Mind Conference Theme
content:
We have been inspired by a podcast interview with Fredrik Pferdt and Dan Harris and were so enthusiastic we read his book What’s Next is Now. Pferdt talks about radical optimism —a way to develop a future ready mind-set as we navigate ambiguity and uncertainty with thoughtful intention. The ideas in this book stimulated our connections to the Habits of Mind. 

In thinking about “what’s next is now”, we decided to build the conference around who we are as individuals and who we are collectively as members of the Habits of Mind community.

Whether a participant is new to Habits of Mind or is an avid believer in the framework, the wide range of sessions will explore how to engage with students, families, colleagues to co-create our future. From the classroom to the system level, we hope to provide opportunities to network with a passionate and highly skilled group of professionals, to inspire and plan for action.

Attend the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference!

April 7-8, 2025 | Rochester, NY
Learn More!

DAY 1: Monday, April 7

Building the Future: Empowering Students with Radical Optimism & Habits of Mind

Today’s sessions explore how to cultivate the Habits of Mind in students, empowering them to become active agents in their own learning. We awaken a canvas of possibilities with our students by equipping them with tools and strategies for developing the Habits of Mind.

Throughout the day, you'll gain valuable skills and exchange ideas in these areas:

  • Identifying powerful classroom strategies: discover engaging activities and real-world examples to help students develop critical thinking, problem-solving, and creativity.
  • Creating a growth mindset: Explore strategies to cultivate a positive learning environment where students embrace challenges and view setbacks as opportunities for growth.
  • Empowering student agency: Learn how to shift the focus from teacher-centered to student-centered learning, giving students ownership over their learning journey.

DAY 2: Tuesday, April 8

Leading From Within: Cultivating Your Inner Strength with Habits of Mind, Movement, and Heart

This day is a journey of self-discovery for leaders at all levels, where we assume leadership is described by a set of behaviors regardless of position. We'll explore how the Habits of Mind can nourish your inner strength, allowing you to lead with greater authenticity and impact.

Throughout the day, you'll gain valuable skills and exchange ideas in these areas:

  • Prioritizing Self-Care: Discover strategies for nurturing your well-being and creating sustainable leadership practices.
  • Cultivating a Culture of Growth: Foster a learning environment where everyone thrives in the school community.
  • Embracing Innovation: Lead courage and adaptability, navigating the evolving educational landscape.

Don't miss this unique opportunity to experience the power of the Habits of Mind firsthand!

Connect with a vibrant community of passionate educators and thought leaders who share your dedication to the Habits of Mind. Discover new strategies for implementing the Habits of Mind in your learning organization and leave feeling inspired and empowered.

Register now!

title: “Failure is Not Always My Fault”: A Transformative Perspective from Dana Lesperance
content:
At the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference, attendees will hear a message that transcends education, touching the core of personal growth and resilience. Dana Lesperance, head of the Community High School of Vermont, will present a session titled Rebuilding Trust with Our Students Through Habits of Mind, where he challenges traditional notions of failure.

His approach redefines how individuals perceive setbacks -- not just as educators or leaders, but as human beings navigating life’s challenges.

"Not to sound disrespectful to students, but (it's true that) 'Failure is not always my fault,'" said Dana when speaking to how he often leads with Taking Responsible Risks when engaging students around the Habits of Mind.

"That’s what our students have experienced so much: failure. But through reflection on Habits of Mind, they learn to say, ‘Not yet. I’ll get there.’"

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WMGLXaOmaSc

Habits That Transform Mindsets

Key to Dana’s philosophy is the idea that Habits of Mind are more than 16 strategies for success—they are a transformative framework for approaching life. Students are encouraged to persist, strive for accuracy, and think flexibly. These habits become a "backpack" of tools they carry with them into every challenge, empowering them to tackle obstacles with confidence.

"When our students can say, ‘I’m using my habits,’ we know we’ve succeeded in building a foundation of trust," said Dana. "Once you’ve established that culture, you’ve built trust—they want to show up for you. They want to show up for themselves."

Why This Message Matters

Dana’s message reaches far beyond the walls of his school. It challenges everyone—educators, administrators, parents, and professionals—to rethink their relationship with failure. Habits of Mind offer a pathway to personal resilience, teaching individuals to reflect, adapt, and grow. These lessons are invaluable in today’s fast-changing world, where setbacks are inevitable, but resilience is a choice.

"Habits of Mind are what tie together the student experience," said Dana. "They allow us to move students forward, no matter their starting point."

The Takeaway for Attendees

Attendees of Dana’s session will walk away with practical strategies for:

  • Using reflection to reframe failure as an opportunity for growth.
  • Building resilience through Habits of Mind, both personally and professionally.
  • Creating environments where individuals feel empowered to take responsible risks and persist.

This session is not just for educators. Anyone seeking tools to foster resilience and personal growth will find Dana’s insights invaluable.

Don't Miss Out!

Last year’s Habits of Mind Conference left attendees inspired to make meaningful changes in their personal and professional lives. This year promises to take those insights even deeper. Dana’s session is one you can’t afford to miss.

Register now to secure your spot at the conference in Greece, New York, this April. Early Bird rates are ending soon -- don’t wait to be part of this transformative experience.

title: How Habits of Mind Are Turning Art Students into Science Enthusiasts
excerpt: Science teacher Lissette Rodriguez uses a weekly sticky note activity where students reflect on how their peers helped them succeed.
content:
In a school where creativity and artistic expression take center stage, science often doesn’t top the list of favorite subjects. At Miami Arts Studio in Miami, Florida, teacher Lissette Rodriguez is flipping that narrative.

By leveraging the Habits of Mind, she’s cultivating a positive attitude toward science in her students, leading them to embrace the subject—not out of obligation, but as a natural result of a transformative learning process.

Lissette teaches biology, anatomy, and AP Biology to students who are primarily focused on the arts. Despite this, she has seen many of her students develop a genuine passion for science and even pursue science-related careers after graduation.

"I teach in an art school, so science is not the strong suit of my students," said Lissette. "But I try to make it the strongest one. By the end, they love science and go into a lot of science careers, just because of that positive mindset."

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w0eyqii6gAk

Encouraging Reflection with Habits of Mind

At the core of her teaching is Metacognition -- Thinking About Your Thinking, which encourages students to reflect on their thoughts, strategies, and feelings, as well as how their actions impact others.

Lissette integrates this habit into her classroom through regular reflection activities that help students develop self-awareness and improve their approach to learning.

"Every Friday, we reflect: How did we do this week? What worked? How can we make it better? How can we fix it?" said Lissette.

This consistent focus on reflection helps students become more intentional in their learning, creating an environment where they feel empowered to tackle challenges and celebrate progress.

Cultivating Curiosity, Confidence, and Persistence

"When you walk into this room, you need to be open," said Lissette. "We’re going to discuss everything, ask questions, and develop a discussion."

This open and exploratory approach aligns seamlessly with the Habits of Mind, fostering a classroom culture that prioritizes curiosity and adaptability. Students are encouraged to persist even when faced with difficulties, and they quickly realize that their struggles are part of the learning process.

"We didn’t get it the first time," said Lissette. "But how can we actually get it? What do we need to change?"

Persisting is also central to Lissette’s teaching philosophy. She encourages her students to analyze their challenges, reflect on their strategies, and try again. This process helps them develop the grit and resilience necessary to succeed—not just in science, but in life.

A Natural Love for Science

The result is transformative. Lissette’s students aren’t just learning science—they’re learning how to engage with the world in a way that opens doors to new possibilities. By reflecting on their thinking and embracing a positive attitude, Lissette’s students are discovering a love for science that feels natural and unforced.

She uses this classroom strategy as a tool:

Lissette uses a simple but powerful activity to foster positivity and collaboration in her classroom:

  1. Set the Scene: Divide students into small groups of 4–5.
  2. Weekly Reflection: At the end of each week, ask students to write a sticky note highlighting something positive a group member did to help them succeed.
  3. Share the Notes: Encourage students to share their notes with their group. This reinforces teamwork and helps students feel recognized and valued.
  4. Impact: "By the end, they love the class and come back for more classes, even hard ones," said Lissette. "They see that they can do it, and they are open to it."

Through her work, Lissette demonstrates how the Habits of Mind can extend beyond academic success to shape students’ perspectives and aspirations.

In her art-focused school, science has become not just a subject but a gateway to a new way of thinking and engaging with the world.

Develop Lifelong Skills At The Habits of Mind Conference

title: Applying the Habits of Mind to Hiring and Decision-Making
excerpt: How Habits of Mind Help Leaders Think Critically, Adapt, and Make Better Decisions
content:
What if the key to hiring the right leaders and decision-makers wasn’t just in their resumes, but in the way they think?

In today’s unpredictable and fast-moving world, organizations need more than just qualified candidates -- they need adaptive thinkers, problem solvers, and reflective decision-makers. The Habits of Mind provides a powerful lens for evaluating not only students and educators, but also leaders, boards, and hiring committees.

During a recent discussion previewing the Habits of Mind Conference, taking place April 7-8 in Rochester, NY, leaders explored how these habits can be integrated into the hiring process and decision-making at all levels of an organization.

Transforming Hiring Practices

Regina McDuffie, Ph.D., head of a preschool-through-eighth-grade school in Southern California, shared how she began applying Habits of Mind to leadership and governance. As she prepared for upcoming interviews, she realized the framework could offer a more structured way to assess candidates.

“As I prepare to interview for some leadership positions here, I've been thinking about the interview questions,” McDuffie said. “I'm thinking about the Habits of Mind, but I never thought about how to get the team that is working on that interview committee to use it in that way of assessing and evaluating in a more structured way.”

Margie Sills-Maerov, a healthcare professional specializing in coaching and team development, reinforced this perspective by sharing how she integrated Habits of Mind into the hiring process for a hospital CEO.

“If somebody walked through my door and had all these skills, I'd hire them in a heartbeat,” Sills-Maerov said. “The wonderful thing is, everybody does have these skills. The question is, how do you start to elevate that and create conditions for people?”

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qatnhmy-TWA

Sills-Maerov developed an interview guide that measured a candidate’s ability by looking for the following Habits of Mind:

  • Thinking Flexibly: Being able to change perspectives; generating alternatives, considering options.
  • Questioning and Posing Problems: Having a questioning attitude; knowing what data are needed and developing questioning strategies to produce those data. Finding problems to solve.
  • Striving for Accuracy: Doing your best. Setting high standards. Fact checking and finding ways to improve.
  • Thinking About Your Thinking (Metacognition): Being aware of your own thoughts, strategies, feelings and actions and their effects on others.

"We overlaid [the hiring process] with Habits of Mind and coaching to find a new CEO for an organization that is on the precipice of huge change," Sills-Maerov said. "By assessing the habits, you were able to assess what kind of thinker this person is and whether they could be adaptive."

Reflection in Leadership Decision-Making

Beyond hiring, educational consultant Michelle Hughes highlighted the importance of applying Habits of Mind at every level of leadership, ensuring decisions are made with deliberation, adaptability, and collective problem-solving.

“The things that Regina shared and the things that Margie shared in terms of how Habits of Mind function in these very divergent ways at every level of an organization really resonated with me,” Hughes said. “You begin with yourself -- yourself as a leader, yourself as a teacher, yourself as a consultant -- and how you reflect and grow your own habits of mind.”

She emphasized that self-awareness among leaders is crucial for fostering a culture where employees feel encouraged to think critically and creatively.

“It’s so authentic in that way, and that’s the trajectory I always bring to my own courses,” Hughes said. “It’s so important.”

A Culture of Inquiry and Continuous Growth

Integrating Habits of Mind into hiring and leadership isn’t just about identifying strong candidates -- it’s about cultivating a culture that encourages ongoing learning and adaptability.

“The accessibility and flexibility of Habits of Mind allows organizations to rethink how they men

title: How Can You Create a Purpose-Driven Classroom with the Habits of Mind?
excerpt: "When students face a challenge, instead of seeing it as a failure, we frame it as an opportunity to apply persistence, flexibility, and problem-solving." -- Dan Vollrath
content:
As educators navigate the challenges of modern classrooms, fostering a learning environment where students develop resilience, self-awareness, and purpose has never been more critical.

Dan Vollrath, a special education teacher and author of The Purpose-Driven Classroom, has dedicated his career to integrating Habits of Mind into daily learning, helping students build not only academic skills but also the mental and emotional strength necessary for lifelong success.

"I always tell myself, if I can start the day with something hard, the rest of the day is going to be easy," said Vollrath. "That mindset helps me stay focused and energized, and it’s the same principle I try to instill in my students."

At the upcoming Habits of Mind Conference (April 7-8, Rochester, NY), Vollrath will lead sessions on student autonomy, emotional intelligence, and building a purpose-driven learning environment. His approach is centered on small, intentional habits that make a significant impact over time.

Bringing Habits of Mind into the Classroom

For Vollrath, the Habits of Mind serve as the foundation for his teaching philosophy.

"It’s not just a lesson—it’s a mindset. We journal about habits, we discuss them in class, and we reflect on them daily," he explained. "I want students to recognize how these habits show up in their lives, not just in school but in the decisions they make outside of it."

In his classroom, students regularly reflect on questions like, What Habit of Mind helped you succeed this week? or Which habit do you need to strengthen? This practice encourages metacognition, helping students recognize their growth and take ownership of their learning.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_DxDrGEenT4

Helping Students Build Resilience

Vollrath emphasizes that resilience is a learned skill, and the way educators design their classrooms plays a crucial role in developing it.

"When students face a challenge, instead of seeing it as a failure, we frame it as an opportunity to apply persistence, flexibility, and problem-solving," he said. "That’s how we help them build resilience—not by avoiding struggles but by equipping them with the tools to navigate them."

His upcoming session, The Purpose-Driven Classroom: Seven Productive Behaviors for Student Success, will explore strategies to help educators embed resilience-building practices into their curriculum.

The Power of Community and Collaboration

Beyond his classroom, Vollrath is passionate about fostering a community of educators who support each other in bringing Habits of Mind to life.

"We learn so much from each other. Some of the best strategies I use in my classroom came from conversations with other teachers," he shared. "At the conference, I’m looking forward to those discussions—the ones that happen not just in sessions but in hallways, over lunch, and after presentations. That’s where the magic happens."

Join the Conversation

Vollrath invites educators to join him at the Habits of Mind Conference to explore how intentional habits shape successful students.

"This work isn’t just about improving test scores—it’s about shaping students into critical thinkers, problem-solvers, and lifelong learners," he said. "If that’s the kind of impact you want to have, I’d love to see you there."

🚀 Don’t miss your chance to learn from Dan Vollrath and other incredible speakers!
📅 Habits of Mind Conference – April 7-8, Rochester, NY
⏳ Early Bird Registration Ends February 14!
🔗 Register now!

title: Radical Optimism at the Habits of Mind Conference: Shaping the Future Through Action
excerpt: Embracing Uncertainty to Shape a Better Tomorrow
content:
Every challenge presents an opportunity. That’s the core of Radical Optimism, the theme of the 2025 Habits of Mind Conference. This powerful concept, inspired by Dr. Fredrik Pferdt, invites us to reframe uncertainty as a chance for growth, learning, and transformation.

The Path to Radical Optimism

The idea for this year’s theme began with a simple yet profound moment. While listening to a podcast featuring Dr. Pferdt, conference co-director Allison Zmuda was struck by his insights into shaping the future with intention.

She immediately ordered his book, What’s Next is Now, and shared its concepts with Bena Kallick, co-director of the Habits of Mind Institute. Together, they recognized a deep connection between Radical Optimism and the Habits of Mind, leading them to weave it into the heart of this year’s conference.

What Does Radical Optimism Mean?

Radical Optimism isn’t about ignoring challenges -- it’s about embracing them as opportunities to create something new. Dr. Pferdt challenges us to shift from asking ‘What will the future bring?’ to ‘What future do I want to create?’ This small but profound change puts us in the driver’s seat of our own learning and development.

The Habits of Mind — such as Thinking Flexibly, Persisting, Managing Impulsivity, and Remaining Open to Continuous Learning -- are essential tools for cultivating this mindset. When applied to education, Radical Optimism empowers students, teachers, and leaders to co-create the future rather than passively accept it.

Conference Highlights

The 2025 Habits of Mind Conference will feature engaging sessions that explore:

  • Practical strategies to build a future-ready mindset
  • How educators can create environments that foster curiosity and adaptability
  • Ways to strengthen psychological safety in classrooms and organizations
  • The role of experimentation in shaping learning and leadership

Engaging Interview with Dr. Fredrik Pferdt

To deepen the conversation, Dr. Pferdt joined Allison and Bena for an exclusive interview, sharing powerful insights on Radical Optimism, adaptability, and the habits that help us navigate an uncertain world.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gRZyGD5ALxs

A Call to Action

The future isn’t something that happens to us -- it’s something we create. By embracing Radical Optimism, we move beyond fear and into action, shaping what’s next with intention and creativity.

Join us for this inspiring event where we take the next step toward a future defined by curiosity, resilience, and learning.

🚀 Don’t miss your chance to experience Radical Optimism!
📅 Habits of Mind Conference – April 7-8, Rochester, NY
🔗 Register now!

title: Leadership, Joy, and Radical Optimism: Joelle Weaver Returns to the Habits of Mind Conference
excerpt: Practical wisdom from the front lines of educational leadership.
content:
Few voices speak about Habits of Mind with as much heart, experience, and impact as Joelle “Joey” Weaver. As principal of an intermediate school in the Honeoye Falls-Lima Central School District in New York, Joey has not only led systemic integration of Habits of Mind across K–12, she’s also been a passionate conference participant, contributor, and advocate.

This year, she returns to the Habits of Mind Conference as a featured presenter, co-leading two dynamic sessions with Adele Pecora:

  • Leadership Perspectives on Cultivating Habits of Mind

  • Finding Joy in the Journey: Sustaining the Work of Habits of Mind

Registration Extended to March 29!

But before taking the stage, Joey was once a first-time attendee ... an experience that deeply affirmed her commitment to the work.

"One of the most powerful things we saw immediately was the sense of community amongst people who had never met each other," said Joey about last year's conference. "Everyone around you is looking at the world through those same 16 dispositions. It’s really hard to make tangible the amount of energy, kindness, love, compassion, connection that you find at a Habits of Mind conference.”

At last year’s event, Joey brought two teachers (seen above): one a long-time leader in the initiative, the other brand new.

The shared takeaway?

The unifying power of common language across vastly different experiences and backgrounds.

“Our students sort of live and breathe the Habits,” Joey shared. “I even have grandparents in our community commenting on Habits that their grandchildren who attend my school talk about.”

This year's theme, Radical Optimism, resonates deeply with Joey.

“Optimism is hope, and hope is what makes all of the Habits of Mind function,” said Joey. “When you're joyous about what you're doing, it's really hard to stop momentum. You get a lot of people on board.”

For Joey, this kind of joy is both a mindset and a leadership approach. Her presentation, Finding Joy in the Journey, is grounded in sustaining meaningful work over time and helping educators tap into the inner joy that drives lasting change.

And when asked what Habit of Mind most connects to Radical Optimism?

“You have to be flexible. You have to constantly be malleable. And optimism ... I think finding joy is where that's at. So I would say if you're Thinking Flexibly and you're looking for joy, you're radically optimistic.”

Whether you're new to Habits of Mind or decades into the work, Joey’s voice is one to learn from -- filled with warmth, wisdom, and an unwavering belief in the transformative power of Habits-based education.

Conference Registration Extended — Now Closing Friday, March 29!

Don’t miss your chance to attend the Habits of Mind Conference in Greece, NY (April 7–9). Join educators and leaders like Joey Weaver for sessions that will refresh your spirit and sharpen your practice.

title: Inside the IPP Cohort Experience: How a Small Learning Community Sparks Global Impact
excerpt: At the Institute for Habits of Mind, the Individual Practitioner Program (IPP) is intentionally designed to foster deep learning, real connection, and personal transformation.
content:
At the Institute for Habits of Mind, the Individual Practitioner Program (IPP) is intentionally designed to foster deep learning, real connection, and personal transformation. Centered around small cohorts, the experience brings together tight-knit groups of educators and leaders who journey together through collaborative reflection, practice, and growth. While professional development can often feel impersonal or one-size-fits-all, the IPP Program offers something entirely different: a transformative, intimate learning journey that creates lasting global connections.

Each cohort begins with more than just introductions. Participants are invited to bring their own contexts, questions, and challenges into the space. Through weekly live sessions, shared readings, and facilitated reflection, members begin to learn from one another as much as from the curriculum. Many alumni describe the cohort as “the most human PD I’ve ever done” and “a space where I finally felt seen as both a teacher and a learner.”

Past cohorts have included educators from across the U.S., as well as participants from international schools and organizations in Europe, Asia, and South America. These diverse perspectives enrich the experience, offering new ways to interpret, adapt, and apply the Habits in varied cultural and institutional settings.

The program culminates in a Celebration of Learning, where participants present their projects and reflect on their personal journeys. For many, it’s a deeply affirming moment—proof that small, intentional communities can spark real change on a global scale.

The next IPP cohort begins July 28, and spaces are limited. If you’re an educator, coach, or organizational leader looking to deepen your practice, build meaningful professional relationships, and become part of a growing global network, this is the program for you. The Habits of Mind aren’t just ideas. They’re actions, decisions, and ways of being, shared and amplified through community. Join us, and help expand this impact, one thoughtful habit at a time.
